sample.title: The Legend of Tarzan
sample.plot: Following his parents' death in Africa, John Clayton has been be raised by an ape, was known by the name Tarzan, but eventually left Africa and for his parents' home in England, along with the woman he fell in love with and married, Jane Porter. He is asked by Belgian King Leopold to go to Africa to see what he has done there to help the country. Initially, he refuses. But an American, George Washington Williams, wants him to accept so he can accompany him. He says that Leopold might be committing all sorts of atrocities to achieve his goal, like slavery. Clayton agrees and his wife insists that she accompany him because she misses Africa. When they arrive, a man named Rom, who works for Leopold, attacks their village and captures Tarzan and Jane. With Washington's help he escapes and sets out to rescue Jane by going across the jungle. Washington joins him despite being told that he might not make it.

sample.title: Greystoke: The Legend of Tarzan, Lord of the Apes
sample.plot: A shipping disaster in the nineteenth century has stranded a man and woman in the wilds of Africa. The lady is pregnant, and gives birth to a son in their tree house. The mother dies soon afterwards. An ape enters the house and kills the father, and a female ape takes the tiny boy as a replacement for her own dead infant, and raises him as her son. Twenty years later, Captaine Phillippe D'Arnot discovers the man who thinks he is an ape. Evidence in the tree house leads him to believe that he is the direct descendant of the Earl of Greystoke, and thus takes it upon himself to return the man to civilization.

sample.title: The Ghost and the Darkness
sample.plot: Sir Robert Beaumont is behind schedule on a railroad in Africa. Enlisting noted engineer John Henry Patterson to right the ship, Beaumont expects results. Everything seems great until the crew discovers the mutilated corpse of the project's foreman, seemingly killed by a lion. After several more attacks, Patterson calls in famed hunter Charles Remington, who has finally met his match in the bloodthirsty lions.

sample.title: Khartoum
sample.plot: After an Egyptian Army, commanded by British officers, is destroyed in a battle in the Sudan in the 1880s, the British government is in a quandary. It does not want to commit a British military force to a foreign war, but they have a commitment to protect the Egyptians in Khartoum. They decide to ask General Charles "Chinese" Gordon (Charlton Heston), something of a folk hero in the Sudan, as he had cleared the area of the slave trade, to arrange for the evacuation. Gordon agrees, but also decides to defend the city against the forces of Mohammed Ahmed el Mahdi (Sir Laurence Olivier), "The Expected One", and tries to force the British to commit troops.

categories.label: european empires pushing into african interiors
categories.description: Late nineteenth-century Africa is being carved up by rival European powers, who send agents, soldiers, and surveyors far beyond the coast to claim land and command trade. What you'll see: river steamers, frontier stations, official missions, flags and uniforms, and long journeys into country newly drawn into imperial designs.

categories.label: chartered companies and kings extracting wealth
categories.description: Rule is often exercised for profit through royal concessions and private syndicates that turn ivory, rubber, minerals, and transport routes into prizes of conquest. What you'll see: trading posts, forced caravans, quotas and porters, company men with broad authority, and violence tied to getting goods out.

categories.label: railways opening routes for conquest
categories.description: Steel tracks, bridges, and telegraph lines bind ports, garrisons, and inland settlements together, making movement of troops and goods faster than older caravan ways. What you'll see: survey camps, engineering works, blasting and bridge-building, work gangs, depots, and the sense of a line being driven through difficult country.

categories.label: british officers ruling through distant posts
categories.description: Imperial power on the ground often rests in the hands of a few officers or administrators stationed far from London, making weighty decisions with limited help. What you'll see: cantonments, mess tables, dispatches, local levies, formal dress in harsh climates, and authority carried by rank, ritual, and reputation.

categories.label: slave raiding and coerced labour persist
categories.description: Even as empires speak of suppressing slavery, many regions still live with slave raiding, captive taking, and labour compelled by force or debt. What you'll see: chained prisoners, armed escorts, devastated villages, bargaining over human bodies, and moral language used beside brutal practice.

categories.label: african polities resisting foreign domination
categories.description: African rulers, armies, and religious movements are not passive backdrop but organised powers defending territory, sovereignty, and their own order against outside rule. What you'll see: fortified towns, massed warriors, banners and drums, negotiations and ultimatums, and battles shaped by local leadership rather than mere imperial initiative.

categories.label: faith and prophecy moving public life
categories.description: Religion is a public force in politics and war, with Christian missions, Islamic learning, and prophetic claims shaping loyalties, legitimacy, and resistance. What you'll see: prayers before campaigns, holy men and preachers, sacred language in councils, and crowds stirred by promises of divine favour.

categories.label: gunboats, rifles, and older weapons together
categories.description: The age brings breech-loading rifles, artillery, steam transport, and telegraphy, yet these coexist with spears, swords, pack caravans, and animal power. What you'll see: Maxim guns and field pieces beside horsemen and foot columns, telegrams arriving at lonely outposts, and hard marches where modern arms do not remove hardship.

categories.label: settler camps amid dangerous landscapes
categories.description: Foreign camps and stations are small islands of ordered routine set within environments that remain difficult to map, traverse, and control. What you'll see: tents, stockades, watchfires, fever-ridden quarters, supplies running short, and constant attention to river crossings, bush, and wild animals.

categories.label: rank and breeding mark social order
categories.description: Victorian ideas of class, breeding, and proper conduct travel with empire, arranging who commands, who serves, and what counts as civilisation. What you'll see: titled names, strict manners, dress codes, servants and bearers, drawing-room expectations carried overseas, and sharp contrasts between metropolitan polish and frontier roughness.
